Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The paper states tomato CYP712G1 as Solyc10g018150.2 in Plasmid construction, and Data availability lists GenBank accession MW384608 for tomato CYP712G1 cDNA.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The title and summary explicitly name the gene/product as tomato cytochrome P450 CYP712G1.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CYP712G1 is directly studied as part of tomato strigolactone biosynthesis throughout the Results.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results and Discussion place CYP712G1 in the conversion from orobanchol toward solanacol via DDH isomers, i.e. the orobanchol-to-solanacol branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: CYP712G1 acts after orobanchol formation, converting it to DDH isomers, so a downstream branch oxidation step is supported by the assays.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Summary and Results state CYP712G1 catalyses double oxidation of orobanchol to three DDH isomers, with modelling/yeast data suggesting hydroxy-orobanchol as likely intermediate.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Transient expression, VIGS accumulation, and yeast microsome assays all support orobanchol as the primary substrate.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper shows formation of three DDH isomers and also hydroxy-orobanchol-like compounds in yeast assays as likely intermediates.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The Summary says one DDH isomer is converted to solanacol, and Results/Discussion support DDH as immediate precursor(s) en route to solanacol.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports CYP712G1 as a required branch enzyme for orobanchol-to-DDH conversion, but does not explicitly call it rate-limiting or a key control step.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The study connects CYP712G1 activity to solanacol biosynthesis, although CYP712G1 itself does not complete the final DDH-to-solanacol step.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Solanacol is treated in the paper as a strigolactone.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The Summary states strigolactones are rhizosphere signalling molecules and phytohormones; solanacol is one of the major tomato root-exudate SLs, so this general function is supported though not tested specifically for solanacol alone.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Solanacol is repeatedly measured in tomato root exudate, i.e. the rhizosphere-facing exudate.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: VIGS of CYP712G1 caused a 12.2-fold increase in orobanchol and decreases in DDH and solanacol; heterologous assays showed conversion of orobanchol to DDH.

Source_Species.reason: The source gene is from tomato.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper identifies tomato as Solanum lycopersicum.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: For cloning, the full-length cDNA was amplified from tomato cv Craigella root cDNA.

Applied_Species.reason: Experiments involved tomato, Nicotiana benthamiana, and yeast.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper explicitly uses Solanum lycopersicum, Nicotiana benthamiana, and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Moneymaker is supported for tomato and WAT11 for yeast strain, but no variety/cultivar is given for N. benthamiana; lumping these as one field is only partially supported.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: RNA-seq results show CYP712G1 expression pattern similar to known SL genes; the known SL genes were induced by P starvation, repressed by P replenishment, and upregulated by GR24, and CYP712G1 is said to show a very similar pattern (Fig. S4c).

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper assesses tomato root expression and exudate phenotype, transient expression in N. benthamiana leaves, and heterologous expression in yeast microsomes.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: CYP712G1 was expressed and assayed in yeast microsomes, but endogenous subcellular localization of the protein in planta was not directly determined.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CYP712G1 is explicitly a cytochrome P450 in the CYP712 family.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper discusses co-expression with SlD27, SlCCD7, SlCCD8 and SlMAX1, but does not demonstrate physical or functional protein-protein interactions.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches are reported in the paper: RNA-seq co-expression, N. benthamiana expression, tomato VIGS, yeast microsome assay, LC-MS/MS, modelling/docking, and mutagenesis of D305/D312.

Omics_Data.reason: Data availability lists RNA-seq SRA accessions PRJNA679261 and PRJNA686071.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These treatments are explicitly described and used in the RNA-seq/co-expression framework and physiological analyses.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper does not report growth/yield penalties, and it explicitly reports feedback changes in upstream SL biosynthetic gene expression upon silencing.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The Discussion explicitly says the findings allow modification of tomato root exudate SL composition and may be applied in breeding for rhizosphere traits in tomato.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion proposes DDH3 as likely solanacol substrate, states another enzyme is likely required for DDH-to-solanacol, shows lower-efficiency use of 2'-epi-orobanchol, and mutagenesis demonstrates Asp305 essential while D312A retains some activity.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly supported by Results: co-expression-based identification, heterologous conversion of orobanchol to DDH via likely hydroxy intermediates, and VIGS causing 12.2-fold orobanchol increase with reduced DDH/solanacol.

corrections.reason: The paper discusses co-expression with SlD27, SlCCD7, SlCCD8 and SlMAX1, but does not demonstrate physical or functional protein-protein interactions.

Enzyme_Name.reason: CCD8 is a known carotenoid cleavage dioxygenase and is referred to as SlCCD8 in the paper; the enzyme class is standard and consistent with the study context.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: SlCCD8 is used throughout as a known strigolactone biosynthetic gene.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: SlCCD8 is used as part of the core upstream SL pathway, before orobanchol formation.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: It is treated as an upstream known SL biosynthetic gene and included in the CL/orobanchol pathway reconstruction.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Within this paper, SlCCD8 is used as a core SL biosynthetic enzyme, bait gene, and positive VIGS control; this description matches its role in the study.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Silencing SlCCD8 reduced downstream SLs, showing it supplies the precursor pool to these metabolites.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The value states 'not established in this paper,' which is faithful because the paper does not establish rate limitation for SlCCD8.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: In this paper, perturbing SlCCD8 affected orobanchol, DDH isomers, and solanacol in root exudate.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These metabolites are strigolactones in the study context.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The Summary states strigolactones are rhizosphere signalling molecules and phytohormones.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: These SLs are quantified in tomato root exudate.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results state that SlCCD8 silencing strongly reduced orobanchol, solanacol and DDH isomers in tomato root exudate.

Source_Species.reason: SlCCD8 is the tomato CCD8 gene.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Tomato is Solanum lycopersicum in the paper.

Applied_Species.reason: SlCCD8 was used in tomato and in transient pathway reconstruction in Nicotiana benthamiana.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The species used are Solanum lycopersicum and Nicotiana benthamiana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Moneymaker is supported for tomato, but no variety is given for N. benthamiana.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results explicitly state SlCCD8 expression was induced by P starvation, repressed by P replenishment, and upregulated by GR24.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper examines SlCCD8 in tomato roots and uses it in N. benthamiana transient pathway reconstruction and as tomato VIGS positive control.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CCD8 belongs to the carotenoid cleavage dioxygenase family, consistent with its naming and established role.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper shows pathway/co-expression relationships with SlD27, SlCCD7, SlMAX1 and CYP712G1, but not protein-protein interactions.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The paper used SlCCD8 in tomato VIGS with RT-qPCR and LC-MS/MS, and in N. benthamiana pathway reconstruction.

Omics_Data.reason: RNA-seq accessions PRJNA679261 and PRJNA686071 are listed in Data availability and include the dataset used for known SL gene expression/co-expression analyses.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These treatments are explicitly reported for the RNA-seq experiment and expression analyses involving SlCCD8.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results report feedback upregulation of SlCCD7, SlMAX1 and SlCYP712G1 and reduced SlD27 expression in SlCCD8-silenced plants.

Other_Important_Info.reason: SlCCD8 is indeed included as bait gene and positive control, not as the main discovery target.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three listed summary points match the Results: positive-control role, strong reduction of SLs upon VIGS, and support for the RNA-seq/co-expression strategy.

corrections.reason: The paper shows pathway/co-expression relationships with SlD27, SlCCD7, SlMAX1 and CYP712G1, but not protein-protein interactions.
